annotations.label: Proposition (truth-bearer)

anchor.snippet: which can be true or false

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated declaration for 'proposition as truth-bearer'; it simply works inside Lean's core Prop sort rather than formalizing this philosophical notion.

annotations.label: Compound proposition via connectives

anchor.snippet: Compound propositions are formed by connecting propositions by logical connectives

annotations.note: This lead-level description has no dedicated Mathlib declaration; the closer technical match (BoundedFormula/IsQF) is recorded against the later 'Well-formed formula' entry.

annotations.label: Classical truth-functional propositional logic

anchor.snippet: having precisely one of two possible truth values

annotations.note: No single Mathlib declaration characterizes classical truth-functional propositional logic as a field; Mathlib simply works inside classical Prop throughout.

annotations.label: Statement (declarative sentence)

anchor.snippet: which are defined as declarative sentences having truth value

annotations.note: No Mathlib declaration formalizes 'declarative sentence' as a linguistic truth-bearer distinct from core Prop.

annotations.label: Example statements

anchor.snippet: Examples of statements might include

annotations.note: Illustrative natural-language examples with no corresponding Mathlib artifact to cite.

annotations.label: Compound sentence

anchor.snippet: Compound sentences are formed from simpler sentences and express relationships among the constituent sentences

annotations.note: No dedicated Mathlib declaration for 'compound sentence' as a linguistic notion.

annotations.label: Example compound sentences

anchor.snippet: Examples of such compound sentences might include

annotations.note: Illustrative examples with no corresponding Mathlib artifact.

annotations.label: Simple/atomic sentence

anchor.snippet: they are called simple sentences

annotations.note: No dedicated Mathlib declaration for 'simple/atomic sentence' as a natural-language notion (compare the technical Atomic-formula entry below).

annotations.label: Sentential connective

anchor.snippet: Sentential connectives are a broader category that includes logical connectives

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the broader linguistic category of 'sentential connective' distinct from logical connectives.

annotations.label: Logical connective

anchor.snippet: is a kind of sentential connective with the characteristic feature

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize 'logical connective' as an object-level concept distinct from the built-in Prop operators ∧, ∨, ¬, →, ↔.

annotations.label: Argument (premises and conclusion)

anchor.snippet: An argument is defined as a pair of things, namely a set of sentences

annotations.note: Mathlib has no bespoke 'Argument' type, but Theory.ModelsBoundedFormula (T ⊨ᵇ φ, 'every model of T realizes φ') generalizes the premises-set/conclusion pairing to first-order theories.

annotations.label: Example argument (modus ponens)

anchor.snippet: The following is an example of an argument within the scope of propositional logic

annotations.note: Worked example; modus ponens itself is just function application in Lean, not a citable standalone Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Valid argument

anchor.snippet: it is necessary that, if all its premises are true, its conclusion is true

annotations.note: T ⊨ᵇ φ ('every model of T realizes φ') is exactly 'if all premises true then conclusion true', generalized from propositional to first-order theories.

annotations.label: Sound argument

anchor.snippet: An argument is sound if, and only if, it is valid and all its premises are true

annotations.note: Soundness (validity plus actually-true premises) is not formalizable as a standalone Mathlib declaration since it depends on the real-world truth of the premises.

annotations.label: Propositional variable

anchor.snippet: which are interpreted as variables representing statements

annotations.note: No dedicated Mathlib decl for 'propositional variable'; FirstOrder.Language's arity-0 relation symbols could stand in generically, but there is no named construct for this specific concept.

annotations.label: Syntactic consequence (inference line)

anchor.snippet: The inference line represents syntactic consequence

annotations.note: Mathlib's ModelTheory library has no syntactic derivability/turnstile relation (no Provable/⊢), so Gentzen-style syntactic consequence is unformalized.

annotations.label: Atomic formula (primitive symbols)

anchor.snippet: a set of primitive symbols, called atomic formulas

annotations.note: IsAtomic (an equality or relation-symbol application) generalizes 'atomic formula' from propositional calculus to arbitrary first-order languages.

annotations.label: Connective (operator symbols)

anchor.snippet: a set of operator symbols, called connectives

annotations.note: BoundedFormula's imp and not constructors (with derived ⊓, ⊔, ↔) generalize propositional connectives to first-order formulas.

annotations.label: Well-formed formula

anchor.snippet: A well-formed formula is any atomic formula, or any formula that can be built up from atomic formulas

annotations.note: IsQF (quantifier-free = Boolean combination of atomic formulas) generalizes the propositional well-formed-formula grammar to first-order languages.

annotations.label: Definition 1: atomic formulas are formulas

anchor.snippet: Atomic propositional variables are formulas

annotations.note: Matches the base-case clause (equal/rel constructors) of the general first-order BoundedFormula inductive definition.

annotations.label: Definition 2: composition of formulas

anchor.snippet: possibly but not necessarily atomic, possibly but not necessarily distinct, formulas

annotations.note: The imp constructor of the BoundedFormula inductive type generalizes this recursive connective-composition clause to first-order syntax.

annotations.label: Definition 3: closure clause

anchor.snippet: Nothing else is a formula

annotations.note: As a Lean inductive type, BoundedFormula automatically satisfies 'nothing else is a formula', generalizing the closure clause to first-order syntax.

annotations.label: Well-formed formula examples

anchor.snippet: we have the following as examples of well-formed formulas

annotations.note: Illustrative examples; no specific Mathlib artifact to cite.

annotations.label: Context-free (BNF) grammar for the language

anchor.snippet: to write a context-free (CF) grammar for the language

annotations.note: Mathlib has a general theory of context-free grammars (Mathlib/Computability/ContextFreeGrammar.lean) but it is never instantiated for this propositional-formula grammar.

annotations.label: Propositional constants, variables, schemata

anchor.snippet: Propositional constants represent some particular proposition

annotations.note: No Mathlib declaration corresponds to schematic propositional constants/schemata as used here.

annotations.label: Truth/falsity constants

anchor.snippet: which always evaluates to True

annotations.note: realize_top and realize_bot show ⊤/⊥ always realize to True/False, generalizing the propositional truth/falsity constants to first-order BoundedFormula.

annotations.label: Bivalence, noncontradiction, excluded middle

anchor.snippet: there are only two semantic values

annotations.note: Bivalence/excluded middle is a foundational assumption of Mathlib's classical logic rather than a stated propositional-calculus theorem in the library.

annotations.label: Interpretation (case)

anchor.snippet: is an assignment of semantic values to each formula

annotations.note: FirstOrder.Language.Structure (paired with BoundedFormula.Realize) generalizes the notion of an interpretation/case assigning semantic values to formulas.

annotations.label: Number of possible interpretations

anchor.snippet: distinct possible interpretations

annotations.note: Fintype.card_fun (card (α → β) = card β ^ card α) gives 2^n interpretations of n atoms as a special case of this general cardinality lemma.

annotations.label: Counterexample

anchor.snippet: a counterexample is defined as a case

annotations.note: No Mathlib declaration named for a falsifying interpretation of an argument.

annotations.label: Truth-functionality of connectives

anchor.snippet: the assumption of the truth-functionality of the connectives

annotations.note: Realize is defined by structural recursion on connectives (realize_inf/realize_sup/realize_imp/realize_not), so connective semantics is truth-functional by construction, generalized to first-order formulas.

annotations.label: Truth table determines connective semantics

anchor.snippet: It is sufficient for determining the semantics of each of these operators

annotations.note: The recursive Realize definition plays the truth-table role (output determined by inputs), but Mathlib has no explicit finite-table object.

annotations.label: Semantics of the five connectives (assignment style)

anchor.snippet: the five connectives are defined as

annotations.note: realize_not/realize_inf/realize_sup/realize_imp/realize_iff give exactly this connective-by-connective semantics, generalized from Prop assignments to first-order Realize.

annotations.label: Functional completeness with minimal connectives

anchor.snippet: a truth-functionally complete system

annotations.note: No functional-completeness or Sheffer-stroke theorem for propositional connectives was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalence vs biconditional

anchor.snippet: distinguish equivalence from the biconditional

annotations.note: Theory.Iff (notation ⇔[T], semantic equivalence) is kept distinct from BoundedFormula's object-level biconditional constructor, mirroring this equivalence/biconditional distinction at the first-order level.

annotations.label: Truth-in-a-case

anchor.snippet: is true under an interpretation

annotations.note: φ.Realize v xs generalizes 'φ is true under interpretation' to first-order formulas.

annotations.label: Falsity-in-a-case

anchor.snippet: is false under an interpretation

annotations.note: realize_not (φ.not.Realize ↔ ¬φ.Realize) generalizes 'φ is false under interpretation'.

annotations.label: Semantic consequence

anchor.snippet: is a semantic consequence

annotations.note: T ⊨ᵇ φ generalizes propositional semantic consequence to first-order theories.

annotations.label: Valid formula (tautology)

anchor.snippet: is logically valid

annotations.note: Logical validity ('true in every case') is the T = ∅ instance of T ⊨ᵇ φ, generalized to first-order theories.

annotations.label: Consistent sentence

anchor.snippet: is consistent if it is true under at least one interpretation

annotations.note: A formula is consistent iff {φ} is satisfiable, i.e. Theory.IsSatisfiable, generalized to first-order theories.

annotations.label: Complete case / consistent case

anchor.snippet: Complete case

annotations.note: Theory.IsComplete/IsMaximal generalize the notion of a complete, consistent case deciding every sentence, to first-order theories.

annotations.label: Classical logic metatheorems (bivalence consequences)

anchor.snippet: For any given interpretation, a given formula is either true or false under it

annotations.note: IsMaximal.mem_or_not_mem (φ ∈ T ∨ φ.not ∈ T for maximal T) generalizes the bivalence fact that every formula is true or false under a given complete case.

annotations.label: Semantic vs syntactic proof systems

anchor.snippet: semantic proof systems rely on semantic consequence

annotations.note: Mathlib's ModelTheory only has semantic apparatus (Realize/IsSatisfiable) with no syntactic derivability relation, so this semantic/syntactic dichotomy is not represented.

annotations.label: Truth table (semantic proof method)

anchor.snippet: A truth table is a semantic proof method used to determine the truth value

annotations.note: No explicit truth-table data structure or algorithm for propositional formulas exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Semantic tableau

anchor.snippet: A semantic tableau is another semantic proof technique that systematically explores the truth of a proposition

annotations.note: No tableau (refutation-tree) proof system is formalized in Mathlib for classical propositional or first-order logic.

annotations.label: Hilbert-style axiomatic system

anchor.snippet: is a set of axioms or assumptions from which other statements

annotations.note: No Hilbert-style axiom system for propositional logic is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Natural deduction (proof method)

anchor.snippet: Natural deduction is a syntactic method of proof that emphasizes the derivation of conclusions from premises

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize natural deduction as an object-level proof calculus; Lean's own tactic framework is used directly instead.

annotations.label: Sequent calculus

anchor.snippet: represents logical deductions as sequences or

annotations.note: The itauto tactic internally implements Dyckhoff's G4ip contraction-free sequent calculus for intuitionistic propositional tautologies, but this is proof-search automation, not a formalized object-level classical sequent calculus as described here.

annotations.label: Validity criterion via truth table

anchor.snippet: all the lines of a truth table come out true

annotations.note: No explicit truth-table validity procedure is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Truth table invalidity example

anchor.snippet: this table shows that

annotations.note: Worked example; no corresponding Mathlib artifact.

annotations.label: Semantic consequence via truth table

anchor.snippet: we can use a truth table to prove that a formula is a semantic consequence

annotations.note: No explicit truth-table-based consequence procedure is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Analytic tableaux

anchor.snippet: a more efficient, but nevertheless mechanical

annotations.note: No analytic-tableaux proof system is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Signed formula

anchor.snippet: where a signed formula is an expression

annotations.note: No signed-formula construct exists in Mathlib's ModelTheory or elsewhere.

annotations.label: Closure of a tableau branch/tree

anchor.snippet: the branch is said to close

annotations.note: No tableau branch/tree closure notion is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Closed tableau proves contradiction/tautology

anchor.snippet: a closed tree is a proof that the original formula

annotations.note: No tableau proof system is formalized in Mathlib, so this soundness-of-closure fact is not stated.

annotations.label: Tableau proof of argument validity

anchor.snippet: one first writes out the set of premise formulas

annotations.note: No tableau-based argument-validity procedure is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Classically valid argument forms

anchor.section: List of classically valid argument forms
anchor.snippet: these tautologies and semantic consequences hold

annotations.note: No single Mathlib declaration catalogues this list of named classical argument forms, though individual instances (e.g. modus ponens) are trivial via Lean's function application.

annotations.label: Natural deduction: rules of proof, no axioms

anchor.snippet: no axioms are used other than these rules

annotations.note: No natural-deduction rule system for propositional logic is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Proof (Suppes–Lemmon notation)

anchor.snippet: is a sequence of lines containing sentences

annotations.note: No Suppes–Lemmon style proof-line formalism exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ten primitive rules of proof

anchor.snippet: There are ten primitive rules of proof

annotations.note: No formalized catalogue of these ten inference rules exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Natural deduction proof example (MTT derivation)

anchor.snippet: which shows that MTT is not a primitive rule

annotations.note: Worked example within an unformalized proof system; no corresponding Mathlib artifact.

annotations.label: Axiomatic proof (tautologies + modus ponens)

anchor.snippet: certain tautologies are taken as self-evident

annotations.note: No Hilbert-style axiomatic proof system for propositional logic is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Frege's six axioms (Begriffsschrift)

anchor.snippet: Frege's system used only implication and negation as connectives

annotations.note: Frege's Begriffsschrift axiom system is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Łukasiewicz's reduction of Frege's axioms

anchor.snippet: the third axiom is superfluous since it can be derived from the preceding two axioms

annotations.note: This historical independence result about Frege's axiom system is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Łukasiewicz's P2 axiom system

anchor.snippet: ukasiewicz is credited

annotations.note: Łukasiewicz's P2 axiom system is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schematic form of P2 axioms

anchor.snippet: using them to generate an infinite set of axioms

annotations.note: Not formalized; no schema-generation construct for P2's axioms exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Proof example in P2

anchor.snippet: As an example, a proof of

annotations.note: Worked example in an unformalized axiom system; no corresponding Mathlib artifact.

annotations.label: Soundness of classical propositional logic

anchor.snippet: it is sound

annotations.note: Mathlib has no syntactic proof-derivability relation for propositional (or first-order) logic, so a soundness theorem (⊢φ → ⊨φ) cannot be, and is not, stated.

annotations.label: Completeness of classical propositional logic

anchor.snippet: It is also complete

annotations.note: Without a syntactic ⊢ relation in Mathlib's ModelTheory library, a completeness theorem (⊨φ → ⊢φ) is not stated.

annotations.label: Compactness theorem

anchor.snippet: A further central result is compactness

annotations.note: The Compactness Theorem for first-order theories (satisfiable iff finitely satisfiable) generalizes propositional compactness, the special case of a language with only nullary relation symbols.

annotations.label: Syntactic consistency coincides with satisfiability

anchor.snippet: syntactic consistency coincides with satisfiability

annotations.note: Requires a syntactic derivability relation, which Mathlib's ModelTheory library lacks; only the semantic side (IsSatisfiable) is formalized.

annotations.label: Decidability of classical propositional logic

anchor.snippet: Classical propositional logic is also decidable

annotations.note: No general decidability-of-propositional-satisfiability metatheorem exists in Mathlib; per-instance Decidable Prop instances do not constitute this result.

annotations.label: NP-completeness of propositional satisfiability

anchor.snippet: Deciding satisfiability of propositional logic formulas is an NP-complete problem

annotations.note: No complexity-theoretic (Cook–Levin/NP-completeness) formalization of SAT exists in Mathlib.
